requests"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2023-01-17T16:45:46Z","updated_at":"2023-01-20T08:49:36Z","closed_at":"2023-01-20T08:49:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/48318","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48318","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48318.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48318.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This implements `keys` and `pairs` for iterators returned by `enumerate`.

This addresses https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47124, enabling e.g.

```julia
julia> findmin(enumerate(Iterators.filter(!=(1), [3,2,1])))
(2, 2)
```

The definition of `pairs` (in addition to `keys`) is required for iterators that have no length defined, as in the above example.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48318/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48318/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1398081359","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48318#issuecomment-1398081359","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48318","id":1398081359,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5TVQdP","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-01-20T08:49:36Z","updated_at":"2023-01-20T08:49:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Closing this since `Enumerate` doesn't align cleanly with the `keys`/`values`/`pairs` interface.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1084213982","pull_request_review_id":1265835968,"id":1084213982,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5An8re","diff_hunk":"@@ -134,88 +138,45 @@ Base.Sort.partialsortperm!
 
 ## Sorting Algorithms
 
-There are currently four sorting algorithms available in base Julia:
+There are currently four sorting algorithms publicly available in base Julia:
 
   * [`InsertionSort`](@ref)
   * [`QuickSort`](@ref)
   * [`PartialQuickSort(k)`](@ref)
   * [`MergeSort`](@ref)
 
-`InsertionSort` is an O(n²) stable sorting algorithm. It is efficient for very small `n`,
-and is used internally by `QuickSort`.
+By default, the `sort` family of functions uses stable sorting algorithms that are fast
+on most inputs. The exact algorithm choice is an implementation detail to allow for
+future performance improvements. Currently, a hybrid of `RadixSort`, `ScratchQuickSort`,
+`InsertionSort`, and `CountingSort` is used based on input type, size, and composition.
+Implementation details are subject to change but currently availible in the extended help
+of `??Base.DEFAULT_STABLE` and the docstrings of internal sorting algorithms listed there.
 
-`QuickSort` is a very fast sorting algorithm with an average-case time complexity of
-O(n log n). `QuickSort` is stable, i.e., elements considered equal will remain in the same
-order. Notice that O(n²) is worst-case complexity, but it gets vanishingly unlikely as the
-pivot selection is randomized.
-
-`PartialQuickSort(k::OrdinalRange)` is similar to `QuickSort`, but the output array is only
-sorted in the range of `k`. For example:
-
-```jldoctest
-julia> x = rand(1:500, 100);
-
-julia> k = 50:100;
-
-julia> s1 = sort(x; alg=QuickSort);
-
-julia> s2 = sort(x; alg=PartialQuickSort(k));
-
-julia> map(issorted, (s1, s2))
-(true, false)
-
-julia> map(x->issorted(x[k]), (s1, s2))
-(true, true)
-
-julia> s1[k] == s2[k]
-true
-```
-
-!!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The `QuickSort` and `PartialQuickSort` algorithms are stable since Julia 1.9.
-
-`MergeSort` is an O(n log n) stable sorting algorithm but is not in-place – it requires a temporary
-array of half the size of the input array – and is typically not quite as fast as `QuickSort`.
-It is the default algorithm for non-numeric data.
-
-The default sorting algorithms are chosen on the basis that they are fast and stable.
-Usually, `QuickSort` is selected, but `InsertionSort` is preferred for small data.
-You can also explicitly specify your preferred algorithm, e.g.
-`sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`.
-
-The mechanism by which Julia picks default sorting algorithms is implemented via the
-`Base.Sort.defalg` function. It allows a particular algorithm to be registered as the
-default in all sorting functions for specific arrays. For example, here is the default
-method from [`sort.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/base/sort.jl):
-
-```julia
-defalg(v::AbstractArray) = DEFAULT_STABLE
-```
-
-You may change the default behavior for specific types by defining new methods for `defalg`.
+You can explicitly specify your preferred algorithm with the `alg` keyword
+(e.g. `sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`) or reconfigure the default sorting algorithm
+for a custom types by adding a specialized method to the `Base.Sort.defalg` function.
 For example, [InlineStrings.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStrings/InlineStrings.jl/blob/v1.3.2/src/InlineStrings.jl#L903)
 defines the following method:
 ```julia
 Base.Sort.defalg(::AbstractArray{<:Union{SmallInlineStrings, Missing}}) = InlineStringSort
 ```
 
 !!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed
-    to be stable since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
+    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed to be stable
+    since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
 
 ## Alternate orderings
 
-By default, `sort` and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare two
-elements in order to determine which should come first. The
-[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining
-alternate orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define
-a [total order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_order) on a set of elements,
-so that for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
-
-* Exactly one of the following is true: `a` is less than `b`, `b` is less than
-  `a`, or `a` and `b` are equal (according to [`isequal`](@ref)).
-* The relation is transitive - if `a` is less than `b` and `b` is less than `c`
-  then `a` is less than `c`.
+By default, `sort`, `searchsorted`, and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare
+two elements in order to determine which should come first. The
+[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining alternate
+orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define a
+[strict partial order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partially_ordered_set#Strict_partial_order).
+To be a strict partial order, for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
+
+* if `a == b`, then `lt(a, b) == false`;
+* `lt(a, b) && lt(b, a) == false`; and
+* if `lt(a, b) && lt(b, c) == true`, then `lt(a, c) == true`","path":"doc/src/base/sort.md","position":171,"original_position":171,"commit_id":"73cdf437350caa9d3a65c64d50e85e86ce8500c4","original_commit_id":"73cdf437350caa9d3a65c64d50e85e86ce8500c4","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Is `==` used in the sorting algorithms?  ## Sorting Algorithms
 
-There are currently four sorting algorithms available in base Julia:
+There are currently four sorting algorithms publicly available in base Julia:
 
   * [`InsertionSort`](@ref)
   * [`QuickSort`](@ref)
   * [`PartialQuickSort(k)`](@ref)
   * [`MergeSort`](@ref)
 
-`InsertionSort` is an O(n²) stable sorting algorithm. It is efficient for very small `n`,
-and is used internally by `QuickSort`.
+By default, the `sort` family of functions uses stable sorting algorithms that are fast
+on most inputs. The exact algorithm choice is an implementation detail to allow for
+future performance improvements. Currently, a hybrid of `RadixSort`, `ScratchQuickSort`,
+`InsertionSort`, and `CountingSort` is used based on input type, size, and composition.
+Implementation details are subject to change but currently availible in the extended help
+of `??Base.DEFAULT_STABLE` and the docstrings of internal sorting algorithms listed there.
 
-`QuickSort` is a very fast sorting algorithm with an average-case time complexity of
-O(n log n). `QuickSort` is stable, i.e., elements considered equal will remain in the same
-order. Notice that O(n²) is worst-case complexity, but it gets vanishingly unlikely as the
-pivot selection is randomized.
-
-`PartialQuickSort(k::OrdinalRange)` is similar to `QuickSort`, but the output array is only
-sorted in the range of `k`. For example:
-
-```jldoctest
-julia> x = rand(1:500, 100);
-
-julia> k = 50:100;
-
-julia> s1 = sort(x; alg=QuickSort);
-
-julia> s2 = sort(x; alg=PartialQuickSort(k));
-
-julia> map(issorted, (s1, s2))
-(true, false)
-
-julia> map(x->issorted(x[k]), (s1, s2))
-(true, true)
-
-julia> s1[k] == s2[k]
-true
-```
-
-!!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The `QuickSort` and `PartialQuickSort` algorithms are stable since Julia 1.9.
-
-`MergeSort` is an O(n log n) stable sorting algorithm but is not in-place – it requires a temporary
-array of half the size of the input array – and is typically not quite as fast as `QuickSort`.
-It is the default algorithm for non-numeric data.
-
-The default sorting algorithms are chosen on the basis that they are fast and stable.
-Usually, `QuickSort` is selected, but `InsertionSort` is preferred for small data.
-You can also explicitly specify your preferred algorithm, e.g.
-`sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`.
-
-The mechanism by which Julia picks default sorting algorithms is implemented via the
-`Base.Sort.defalg` function. It allows a particular algorithm to be registered as the
-default in all sorting functions for specific arrays. For example, here is the default
-method from [`sort.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/base/sort.jl):
-
-```julia
-defalg(v::AbstractArray) = DEFAULT_STABLE
-```
-
-You may change the default behavior for specific types by defining new methods for `defalg`.
+You can explicitly specify your preferred algorithm with the `alg` keyword
+(e.g. `sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`) or reconfigure the default sorting algorithm
+for a custom types by adding a specialized method to the `Base.Sort.defalg` function.
 For example, [InlineStrings.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStrings/InlineStrings.jl/blob/v1.3.2/src/InlineStrings.jl#L903)
 defines the following method:
 ```julia
 Base.Sort.defalg(::AbstractArray{<:Union{SmallInlineStrings, Missing}}) = InlineStringSort
 ```
 
 !!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed
-    to be stable since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
+    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed to be stable
+    since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
 
 ## Alternate orderings
 
-By default, `sort` and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare two
-elements in order to determine which should come first. The
-[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining
-alternate orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define
-a [total order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_order) on a set of elements,
-so that for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
-
-* Exactly one of the following is true: `a` is less than `b`, `b` is less than
-  `a`, or `a` and `b` are equal (according to [`isequal`](@ref)).
-* The relation is transitive - if `a` is less than `b` and `b` is less than `c`
-  then `a` is less than `c`.
+By default, `sort`, `searchsorted`, and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare
+two elements in order to determine which should come first. The
+[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining alternate
+orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define a

 ## Sorting Algorithms
 
-There are currently four sorting algorithms available in base Julia:
+There are currently four sorting algorithms publicly available in base Julia:
 
   * [`InsertionSort`](@ref)
   * [`QuickSort`](@ref)
   * [`PartialQuickSort(k)`](@ref)
   * [`MergeSort`](@ref)
 
-`InsertionSort` is an O(n²) stable sorting algorithm. It is efficient for very small `n`,
-and is used internally by `QuickSort`.
+By default, the `sort` family of functions uses stable sorting algorithms that are fast
+on most inputs. The exact algorithm choice is an implementation detail to allow for
+future performance improvements. Currently, a hybrid of `RadixSort`, `ScratchQuickSort`,
+`InsertionSort`, and `CountingSort` is used based on input type, size, and composition.
+Implementation details are subject to change but currently availible in the extended help
+of `??Base.DEFAULT_STABLE` and the docstrings of internal sorting algorithms listed there.
 
-`QuickSort` is a very fast sorting algorithm with an average-case time complexity of
-O(n log n). `QuickSort` is stable, i.e., elements considered equal will remain in the same
-order. Notice that O(n²) is worst-case complexity, but it gets vanishingly unlikely as the
-pivot selection is randomized.
-
-`PartialQuickSort(k::OrdinalRange)` is similar to `QuickSort`, but the output array is only
-sorted in the range of `k`. For example:
-
-```jldoctest
-julia> x = rand(1:500, 100);
-
-julia> k = 50:100;
-
-julia> s1 = sort(x; alg=QuickSort);
-
-julia> s2 = sort(x; alg=PartialQuickSort(k));
-
-julia> map(issorted, (s1, s2))
-(true, false)
-
-julia> map(x->issorted(x[k]), (s1, s2))
-(true, true)
-
-julia> s1[k] == s2[k]
-true
-```
-
-!!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The `QuickSort` and `PartialQuickSort` algorithms are stable since Julia 1.9.
-
-`MergeSort` is an O(n log n) stable sorting algorithm but is not in-place – it requires a temporary
-array of half the size of the input array – and is typically not quite as fast as `QuickSort`.
-It is the default algorithm for non-numeric data.
-
-The default sorting algorithms are chosen on the basis that they are fast and stable.
-Usually, `QuickSort` is selected, but `InsertionSort` is preferred for small data.
-You can also explicitly specify your preferred algorithm, e.g.
-`sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`.
-
-The mechanism by which Julia picks default sorting algorithms is implemented via the
-`Base.Sort.defalg` function. It allows a particular algorithm to be registered as the
-default in all sorting functions for specific arrays. For example, here is the default
-method from [`sort.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/base/sort.jl):
-
-```julia
-defalg(v::AbstractArray) = DEFAULT_STABLE
-```
-
-You may change the default behavior for specific types by defining new methods for `defalg`.
+You can explicitly specify your preferred algorithm with the `alg` keyword
+(e.g. `sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`) or reconfigure the default sorting algorithm
+for a custom types by adding a specialized method to the `Base.Sort.defalg` function.
 For example, [InlineStrings.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStrings/InlineStrings.jl/blob/v1.3.2/src/InlineStrings.jl#L903)
 defines the following method:
 ```julia
 Base.Sort.defalg(::AbstractArray{<:Union{SmallInlineStrings, Missing}}) = InlineStringSort
 ```
 
 !!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed
-    to be stable since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
+    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed to be stable
+    since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
 
 ## Alternate orderings
 
-By default, `sort` and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare two
-elements in order to determine which should come first. The
-[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining
-alternate orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define
-a [total order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_order) on a set of elements,
-so that for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
-
-* Exactly one of the following is true: `a` is less than `b`, `b` is less than
-  `a`, or `a` and `b` are equal (according to [`isequal`](@ref)).
-* The relation is transitive - if `a` is less than `b` and `b` is less than `c`
-  then `a` is less than `c`.
+By default, `sort`, `searchsorted`, and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare
+two elements in order to determine which should come first. The
+[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining alternate
+orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define a
+[strict partial order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partially_ordered_set#Strict_partial_order).
+To be a strict partial order, for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
+
+* if `a == b`, then `lt(a, b) == false`;
+* `lt(a, b) && lt(b, a) == false`; and
+* if `lt(a, b) && lt(b, c) == true`, then `lt(a, c) == true`","path":"doc/src/base/sort.md","position":171,"original_position":171,"commit_id":"73cdf437350caa9d3a65c64d50e85e86ce8500c4","original_commit_id":"73cdf437350caa9d3a65c64d50e85e86ce8500c4","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Then maybe the first point should read like the following?

 ## Sorting Algorithms
 
-There are currently four sorting algorithms available in base Julia:
+There are currently four sorting algorithms publicly available in base Julia:
 
   * [`InsertionSort`](@ref)
   * [`QuickSort`](@ref)
   * [`PartialQuickSort(k)`](@ref)
   * [`MergeSort`](@ref)
 
-`InsertionSort` is an O(n²) stable sorting algorithm. It is efficient for very small `n`,
-and is used internally by `QuickSort`.
+By default, the `sort` family of functions uses stable sorting algorithms that are fast
+on most inputs. The exact algorithm choice is an implementation detail to allow for
+future performance improvements. Currently, a hybrid of `RadixSort`, `ScratchQuickSort`,
+`InsertionSort`, and `CountingSort` is used based on input type, size, and composition.
+Implementation details are subject to change but currently availible in the extended help
+of `??Base.DEFAULT_STABLE` and the docstrings of internal sorting algorithms listed there.
 
-`QuickSort` is a very fast sorting algorithm with an average-case time complexity of
-O(n log n). `QuickSort` is stable, i.e., elements considered equal will remain in the same
-order. Notice that O(n²) is worst-case complexity, but it gets vanishingly unlikely as the
-pivot selection is randomized.
-
-`PartialQuickSort(k::OrdinalRange)` is similar to `QuickSort`, but the output array is only
-sorted in the range of `k`. For example:
-
-```jldoctest
-julia> x = rand(1:500, 100);
-
-julia> k = 50:100;
-
-julia> s1 = sort(x; alg=QuickSort);
-
-julia> s2 = sort(x; alg=PartialQuickSort(k));
-
-julia> map(issorted, (s1, s2))
-(true, false)
-
-julia> map(x->issorted(x[k]), (s1, s2))
-(true, true)
-
-julia> s1[k] == s2[k]
-true
-```
-
-!!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The `QuickSort` and `PartialQuickSort` algorithms are stable since Julia 1.9.
-
-`MergeSort` is an O(n log n) stable sorting algorithm but is not in-place – it requires a temporary
-array of half the size of the input array – and is typically not quite as fast as `QuickSort`.
-It is the default algorithm for non-numeric data.
-
-The default sorting algorithms are chosen on the basis that they are fast and stable.
-Usually, `QuickSort` is selected, but `InsertionSort` is preferred for small data.
-You can also explicitly specify your preferred algorithm, e.g.
-`sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`.
-
-The mechanism by which Julia picks default sorting algorithms is implemented via the
-`Base.Sort.defalg` function. It allows a particular algorithm to be registered as the
-default in all sorting functions for specific arrays. For example, here is the default
-method from [`sort.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/base/sort.jl):
-
-```julia
-defalg(v::AbstractArray) = DEFAULT_STABLE
-```
-
-You may change the default behavior for specific types by defining new methods for `defalg`.
+You can explicitly specify your preferred algorithm with the `alg` keyword
+(e.g. `sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`) or reconfigure the default sorting algorithm
+for a custom types by adding a specialized method to the `Base.Sort.defalg` function.
 For example, [InlineStrings.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStrings/InlineStrings.jl/blob/v1.3.2/src/InlineStrings.jl#L903)
 defines the following method:
 ```julia
 Base.Sort.defalg(::AbstractArray{<:Union{SmallInlineStrings, Missing}}) = InlineStringSort
 ```
 
 !!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed
-    to be stable since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
+    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed to be stable
+    since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
 
 ## Alternate orderings
 
-By default, `sort` and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare two
-elements in order to determine which should come first. The
-[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining
-alternate orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define
-a [total order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_order) on a set of elements,
-so that for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
-
-* Exactly one of the following is true: `a` is less than `b`, `b` is less than
-  `a`, or `a` and `b` are equal (according to [`isequal`](@ref)).
-* The relation is transitive - if `a` is less than `b` and `b` is less than `c`
-  then `a` is less than `c`.
+By default, `sort`, `searchsorted`, and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare
+two elements in order to determine which should come first. The
+[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining alternate
+orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define a

I don't think that's right, for example consider the following (invalid) order on complex numbers:

```
lt(a,b) = isreal(a) && isreal(b) ? a<b : abs(a)<abs(b)
```

Then with `a, b, c = 1, im, -1`:

```
julia> !lt(a,b) && !lt(b,c)
true

julia> !lt(a,c)
true
```

but

```
incomparable(a,b) = !lt(a,b) && !lt(b,a)

julia> incomparable(a,b) && incomparable(b,c)
true

julia> incomparable(a,c)
false
```

I think even if we find a way to reduce the conditions to something simpler, it's probably a bad idea: better give the conditions that are standard in the literature and that make the point clear, for example here the point is "transitiveness of incomparability", which would not be obvious if we gave a kind of reduced  ## Sorting Algorithms
 
-There are currently four sorting algorithms available in base Julia:
+There are currently four sorting algorithms publicly available in base Julia:
 
   * [`InsertionSort`](@ref)
   * [`QuickSort`](@ref)
   * [`PartialQuickSort(k)`](@ref)
   * [`MergeSort`](@ref)
 
-`InsertionSort` is an O(n²) stable sorting algorithm. It is efficient for very small `n`,
-and is used internally by `QuickSort`.
+By default, the `sort` family of functions uses stable sorting algorithms that are fast
+on most inputs. The exact algorithm choice is an implementation detail to allow for
+future performance improvements. Currently, a hybrid of `RadixSort`, `ScratchQuickSort`,
+`InsertionSort`, and `CountingSort` is used based on input type, size, and composition.
+Implementation details are subject to change but currently availible in the extended help
+of `??Base.DEFAULT_STABLE` and the docstrings of internal sorting algorithms listed there.
 
-`QuickSort` is a very fast sorting algorithm with an average-case time complexity of
-O(n log n). `QuickSort` is stable, i.e., elements considered equal will remain in the same
-order. Notice that O(n²) is worst-case complexity, but it gets vanishingly unlikely as the
-pivot selection is randomized.
-
-`PartialQuickSort(k::OrdinalRange)` is similar to `QuickSort`, but the output array is only
-sorted in the range of `k`. For example:
-
-```jldoctest
-julia> x = rand(1:500, 100);
-
-julia> k = 50:100;
-
-julia> s1 = sort(x; alg=QuickSort);
-
-julia> s2 = sort(x; alg=PartialQuickSort(k));
-
-julia> map(issorted, (s1, s2))
-(true, false)
-
-julia> map(x->issorted(x[k]), (s1, s2))
-(true, true)
-
-julia> s1[k] == s2[k]
-true
-```
-
-!!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The `QuickSort` and `PartialQuickSort` algorithms are stable since Julia 1.9.
-
-`MergeSort` is an O(n log n) stable sorting algorithm but is not in-place – it requires a temporary
-array of half the size of the input array – and is typically not quite as fast as `QuickSort`.
-It is the default algorithm for non-numeric data.
-
-The default sorting algorithms are chosen on the basis that they are fast and stable.
-Usually, `QuickSort` is selected, but `InsertionSort` is preferred for small data.
-You can also explicitly specify your preferred algorithm, e.g.
-`sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`.
-
-The mechanism by which Julia picks default sorting algorithms is implemented via the
-`Base.Sort.defalg` function. It allows a particular algorithm to be registered as the
-default in all sorting functions for specific arrays. For example, here is the default
-method from [`sort.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/base/sort.jl):
-
-```julia
-defalg(v::AbstractArray) = DEFAULT_STABLE
-```
-
-You may change the default behavior for specific types by defining new methods for `defalg`.
+You can explicitly specify your preferred algorithm with the `alg` keyword
+(e.g. `sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`) or reconfigure the default sorting algorithm
+for a custom types by adding a specialized method to the `Base.Sort.defalg` function.
 For example, [InlineStrings.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStrings/InlineStrings.jl/blob/v1.3.2/src/InlineStrings.jl#L903)
 defines the following method:
 ```julia
 Base.Sort.defalg(::AbstractArray{<:Union{SmallInlineStrings, Missing}}) = InlineStringSort
 ```
 
 !!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed
-    to be stable since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
+    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed to be stable
+    since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
 
 ## Alternate orderings
 
-By default, `sort` and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare two
-elements in order to determine which should come first. The
-[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining
-alternate orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define
-a [total order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_order) on a set of elements,
-so that for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
-
-* Exactly one of the following is true: `a` is less than `b`, `b` is less than
-  `a`, or `a` and `b` are equal (according to [`isequal`](@ref)).
-* The relation is transitive - if `a` is less than `b` and `b` is less than `c`
-  then `a` is less than `c`.
+By default, `sort`, `searchsorted`, and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare
+two elements in order to determine which should come first. The
+[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining alternate
+orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define a
+[strict partial order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partially_ordered_set#Strict_partial_order).
+To be a strict partial order, for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
+
+* if `a == b`, then `lt(a, b) == false`;
+* `lt(a, b) && lt(b, a) == false`; and

 """
     sort!(v; alg::Algorithm=defalg(v), lt=isless, by=identity, rev::Bool=false, order::Ordering=Forward)
 
-Sort the vector `v` in place. A stable algorithm is used by default. You can select a
-specific algorithm to use via the `alg` keyword (see [Sorting Algorithms](@ref) for
-available algorithms). The `by` keyword lets you provide a function that will be applied to
-each element before comparison; the `lt` keyword allows providing a custom "less than"
-function (note that for every `x` and `y`, only one of `lt(x,y)` and `lt(y,x)` can return
-`true`); use `rev=true` to reverse the sorting order. These options are independent and can
-be used together in all possible combinations: if both `by` and `lt` are specified, the `lt`
-function is applied to the result of the `by` function; `rev=true` reverses whatever
-ordering specified via the `by` and `lt` keywords.
+Sort the vector `v` in place. A stable algorithm is used by default. A specific
+algorithm can be selected via the `alg` keyword (see [Sorting Algorithms](@ref)
+for available algorithms). Elements are first transformed by the function `by`
+and then compared according to either the function `lt` or the ordering
+`order`. Finally, the resulting order is reversed if `rev=true`.
+
+The `lt` function should define a strict partial order, that is, it should be 
+
+- irreflexive: `lt(x, x)` always yields `false`,
+- asymmetric: if `lt(x, y)` yields `true` then `lt(y, x)` yields `false`,
+- transitive: `lt(x, y) && lt(y, z)` implies `lt(x, Sort the vector `v` in place. [...]

...
"""
    sort!(A; dims::Integer, alg::Algorithm=defalg(A), lt=isless, by=identity, rev::Bool=false, order::Ordering=Forward)

Sort the multidimensional array `A` along dimension `dims`.
```

 ## Sorting Algorithms
 
-There are currently four sorting algorithms available in base Julia:
+There are currently four sorting algorithms publicly available in base Julia:
 
   * [`InsertionSort`](@ref)
   * [`QuickSort`](@ref)
   * [`PartialQuickSort(k)`](@ref)
   * [`MergeSort`](@ref)
 
-`InsertionSort` is an O(n²) stable sorting algorithm. It is efficient for very small `n`,
-and is used internally by `QuickSort`.
+By default, the `sort` family of functions uses stable sorting algorithms that are fast
+on most inputs. The exact algorithm choice is an implementation detail to allow for
+future performance improvements. Currently, a hybrid of `RadixSort`, `ScratchQuickSort`,
+`InsertionSort`, and `CountingSort` is used based on input type, size, and composition.
+Implementation details are subject to change but currently availible in the extended help
+of `??Base.DEFAULT_STABLE` and the docstrings of internal sorting algorithms listed there.
 
-`QuickSort` is a very fast sorting algorithm with an average-case time complexity of
-O(n log n). `QuickSort` is stable, i.e., elements considered equal will remain in the same
-order. Notice that O(n²) is worst-case complexity, but it gets vanishingly unlikely as the
-pivot selection is randomized.
-
-`PartialQuickSort(k::OrdinalRange)` is similar to `QuickSort`, but the output array is only
-sorted in the range of `k`. For example:
-
-```jldoctest
-julia> x = rand(1:500, 100);
-
-julia> k = 50:100;
-
-julia> s1 = sort(x; alg=QuickSort);
-
-julia> s2 = sort(x; alg=PartialQuickSort(k));
-
-julia> map(issorted, (s1, s2))
-(true, false)
-
-julia> map(x->issorted(x[k]), (s1, s2))
-(true, true)
-
-julia> s1[k] == s2[k]
-true
-```
-
-!!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The `QuickSort` and `PartialQuickSort` algorithms are stable since Julia 1.9.
-
-`MergeSort` is an O(n log n) stable sorting algorithm but is not in-place – it requires a temporary
-array of half the size of the input array – and is typically not quite as fast as `QuickSort`.
-It is the default algorithm for non-numeric data.
-
-The default sorting algorithms are chosen on the basis that they are fast and stable.
-Usually, `QuickSort` is selected, but `InsertionSort` is preferred for small data.
-You can also explicitly specify your preferred algorithm, e.g.
-`sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`.
-
-The mechanism by which Julia picks default sorting algorithms is implemented via the
-`Base.Sort.defalg` function. It allows a particular algorithm to be registered as the
-default in all sorting functions for specific arrays. For example, here is the default
-method from [`sort.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/base/sort.jl):
-
-```julia
-defalg(v::AbstractArray) = DEFAULT_STABLE
-```
-
-You may change the default behavior for specific types by defining new methods for `defalg`.
+You can explicitly specify your preferred algorithm with the `alg` keyword
+(e.g. `sort!(v, alg=PartialQuickSort(10:20))`) or reconfigure the default sorting algorithm
+for a custom types by adding a specialized method to the `Base.Sort.defalg` function.
 For example, [InlineStrings.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStrings/InlineStrings.jl/blob/v1.3.2/src/InlineStrings.jl#L903)
 defines the following method:
 ```julia
 Base.Sort.defalg(::AbstractArray{<:Union{SmallInlineStrings, Missing}}) = InlineStringSort
 ```
 
 !!! compat "Julia 1.9"
-    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed
-    to be stable since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
+    The default sorting algorithm (returned by `Base.Sort.defalg`) is guaranteed to be stable
+    since Julia 1.9. Previous versions had unstable edge cases when sorting numeric arrays.
 
 ## Alternate orderings
 
-By default, `sort` and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare two
-elements in order to determine which should come first. The
-[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining
-alternate orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define
-a [total order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_order) on a set of elements,
-so that for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
-
-* Exactly one of the following is true: `a` is less than `b`, `b` is less than
-  `a`, or `a` and `b` are equal (according to [`isequal`](@ref)).
-* The relation is transitive - if `a` is less than `b` and `b` is less than `c`
-  then `a` is less than `c`.
+By default, `sort`, `searchsorted`, and related functions use [`isless`](@ref) to compare
+two elements in order to determine which should come first. The
+[`Base.Order.Ordering`](@ref) abstract type provides a mechanism for defining alternate
+orderings on the same set of elements. Instances of `Ordering` define a
+[strict weak order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weak_ordering#Strict_weak_orderings).
+To be a strict weak order, for any elements `a`, `b`, `c` the following hold:
+
+* `lt(a, b) && lt(b, a) === false`;
+* if `lt(a, b) && lt(b, c)`, then `lt(a, c)`; and
+* if `!lt(a, b) && !lt(b, c)`, then `!lt(a, c)`
 
 The [`Base.Order.lt`](@ref) function works as a generalization of `isless` to

 end
 
 """
-    searchsorted(a, x; by=<transform>, lt=<comparison>, rev=false)
+    searchsorted(v, x; by=identity, lt=isless, rev=false)
+
+Return the range of indices in `v` where values are equivalent to `x`, or an
+empty range located at the insertion point if `v` does not contain values
+equivalent to `x` (see below for the definition of equivalence).
+
+The range is found using binary search. The vector `v` must be sorted, or at
+least partitioned with respect to `x` such that all values that sort before `x`
+come first and all values that sort after `x` come last.
+
+The `by`, `lt` and `rev` keywords modify what order is assumed for the data,
+as described in the [`sort!`](@ref) documentation.
 
-Return the range of indices of `a` which compare as equal to `x` (using binary search)
-according to the order specified by the `by`, `lt` and `rev` keywords, assuming that `a`
-is already sorted in that order. Return an empty range located at the insertion point
-if `a` does not contain values equal to `x`.
+Values `x` and `y` are said equivalent if `lt(by(x), by(y))` and `lt(by(y),
+by(x))` both return `false`, and `x` is said to sort before `y` if `lt(by(x),

 """
     sort!(v; alg::Algorithm=defalg(v), lt=isless, by=identity, rev::Bool=false, order::Ordering=Forward)
 
-Sort the vector `v` in place. A stable algorithm is used by default. You can select a
-specific algorithm to use via the `alg` keyword (see [Sorting Algorithms](@ref) for
-available algorithms). The `by` keyword lets you provide a function that will be applied to
-each element before comparison; the `lt` keyword allows providing a custom "less than"
-function (note that for every `x` and `y`, only one of `lt(x,y)` and `lt(y,x)` can return
-`true`); use `rev=true` to reverse the sorting order. These options are independent and can
-be used together in all possible combinations: if both `by` and `lt` are specified, the `lt`
-function is applied to the result of the `by` function; `rev=true` reverses whatever
-ordering specified via the `by` and `lt` keywords.
+Sort the vector `v` in place. A stable algorithm is used by default. A specific
+algorithm can be selected via the `alg` keyword (see [Sorting Algorithms](@ref)
+for available algorithms). Elements are first transformed by the function `by`
+and then compared according to either the function `lt` or the ordering
+`order`. Finally, the resulting order is reversed if `rev=true`.

particular:

* document the `order` keyword in `sort!`,
* list explicitly the required properties of `lt` in `sort!`,
* clarify the sequence of "by" transformations if both `by` and `order` are given,
* show default values in the signatures for `searchsorted` and related functions,
* add `isunordered` to the manual (it's already exported),
* mention in `sort!` the current behavior of calling `by` functions for each comparison rather than once per element.

I think this fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/2766, https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44102 and https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34947.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48387/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48387/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1403713706","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48387#issuecomment-1403713706","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48387","id":1403713706,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Tqviq","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-01-25T14:32:05Z","updated_at":"2023-01-25T14:32:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Thanks @LilithHafner for the review. changes that should be backported to 1.9 from #48387 and #48363.

Part of an effort to break up those two PRs into smaller chunks so that they can merge more easily, starting with backports so that the backporting process can be more automatic.

- Fix objective typos
- Clarify that `!` means mutation, not "in-place-ness". This should be backported because `sort!` is even less in place in 1.9 than it already was in 1.8.
- Rewrite the section on default policy to reflect the new default policy
- Move examples and extended description of previously default sorting algorithms out of sort.md and into their respective docstrings (still rendered in sort.md)

Requesting review from @knuesel, an author of some of these changes.

This is intended to update the documentation for everything that changed in 1.9 and therefore fixes {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714","id":1465140891,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5XVEab","number":47714,"title":"`splat` rename 1.8 -> 1.9","user":{"login":"aplavin","id":687995,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4Nzk5NQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/687995?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin","html_url":"https://github.com/aplavin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":12,"created_at":"2022-11-26T12:23:53Z","updated_at":"2022-12-28T14:34:59Z","closed_at":"2022-11-29T22:27:11Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In Julia 1.9:
```
help?> Base.Splat
  Splat(f)
<...>
  │ Julia 1.9
  │
  │  This function was introduced in Julia 1.9, replacing Base.splat(f).
```
Meanwhile, `Base.splat` remains, and seems equivalent to `Base.Splat` - but not documented. It's hard to see the rationale for changing the name to capital case.

The current situation is that `Base.splat` is available in both earlier julia versions and in 1.9, while `Base.Splat` only in 1.9. Given that, I find it hard to imagine someone using `Base.Splat` -- `Base.splat` has the same behavior and remains compatible with 1.8-.

Maybe, while 1.9 is not released yet, `Base.splat` should be documented as the official function splatting instead of `Base.Splat`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1366697139","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714#issuecomment-1366697139","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47714","id":1366697139,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5RdiSz","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-12-28T14:34:59Z","updated_at":"2022-12-28T14:34:59Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"@brenhinkeller, do you mean that I can make a PR to restore `splat` as non-deprecated API (and have `Splat` being simply the new way it's implemented internally)?

I would be happy to make such a PR if there's a chance to have it merged. It seems very natural to keep `splat` (now exported) and have it return a `Splat`. It's similar to many parts of the standard library, for example

* `∘` used to return a lambda before 1.6 but now it returns an instance of `ComposedFunction`
* The functions `zip`, `filter`, `flatten`, etc in `Iterators` return corresponding structs `Zip`, `Filter`, `Flatten`, ...
* `range(...)` returns a struct such as `UnitRange`, `StepRange`, etc.

 I agree with @aplavin: this rename is unnecessary and is just going to add confusion. We will forever have a mix of `Base.splat` and `Splat` in the wild and users will wonder what's the difference.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1366697139/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":414,"created_at":"2017-12-08T19:10:10Z","updated_at":"2022-12-30T11:01:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/24990","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR addresses #554, #5571, and #22710 by "currying" underscores in function calls like `f(_,y)` into anonymous function expressions `x -> f(x,y)`.  (Note that `_.foo` works and turns into `x -> x.foo` since it is equivalent to a `getfield` call, and `_[i]` works and turns into `x -> x[i]`, since it is equivalent to a `getindex(_,i)` call.)

This will help us get rid of functions like `equalto` (#23812) or `occursin` (#24967), is useful for "destructuring" as discussed in #22710, and should generally be convenient in lots of cases to avoid having to explicitly do `x -> f(x,y)`.

Some simplifying design decisions that I made:

* The currying is "tight", i.e. it only converts the immediately surrounding function call into a lambda, [as suggested by](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22710#issuecomment-314259250) @JeffBezanson (and [as in Scala](https://www.scala-lang.org/files/archive/spec/2.11/06-expressions.html#placeholder-syntax-for-anonymous-functions)).   So, e.g. `f(g(_,y))` is equivalent to `f(x -> g(x,y))`.   (Note that something like `find(!(_ in c), y)` will work fine, because the `!` operator works on functions; you can also use `_ ∉ c`.)  Any other rule seems hard to make comprehensible and consistent.

* ~~Only a single underscore is allowed.  `f(_,_)` throws an error: this case seems ambiguous to me (do you want `x -> f(x,x)` or `x,y -> f(x,y)`?), so it seemed better to punt on this for now.  We can always add a meaning for multiple underscores later.~~ Similar to Scala, multiple underscores are converted into multiple arguments in the order they appear.  e.g. `f(_,y,_)` is equivalent to `(x,z) -> f(x,y,z)`.  See [rationale below](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-350879561).

The implementation is pretty trivial.  If people are in favor, I will add
- [x] Tests
- [x] Documentation
- [ ] Fix interaction with broadcasting `f.(x, _)`
- [ ] Fix interaction with keyword arguments `f(x; y=_)`
- [ ] Support chained comparisons, e.g. `3 ≤ _ ≤ 10`, since they parse as a single expression?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/reactions","total_count":274,"+1":161,"-1":4,"laugh":0,"hooray":11,"confused":2,"heart":83,"rocket":9,"eyes":4},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1367858081","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-1367858081","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990","id":1367858081,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Rh9uh","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-12-30T11:01:15Z","updated_at":"2022-12-30T11:01:15Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Happy to oblige! Here are my arguments against composition fallback.

1. It's too magical, too much implicit behavior. Hard to reason about and debug when something goes wrong. I know this is vague, but the whole idea of "fallback" gives me the creeps for these reasons.

1. In the words of @stevengj when he proposed this idea (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-350728180):

   > But even this would be extremely tricky to make work properly.
   > 
   > In general, when an anonymous function gets more complicated than a single function call, I doubt that we gain much from any syntax more terse than x ->, especially if the price for terseness is complicated semantics.

1. The tight rule is super readable because the effect of `_` is strictly local. With composition fallback this is no longer true, we can have monster expressions that have a completely different meaning when we flip an `x` to an `_` deep inside.
1. It's very confusing that some things work and some don't, depending on which functions happen to have a method that accepts a function. For example, unless I missed something `filter(_%3==0, 0:10)` would actually not work! That's because `(x->x%3) == 0` is defined (it returns `false`). It could be made to work by manually defining `==(::Fix, x)` but that's breaking (or at least very confusing) because `==(::Function, x)` used to return a boolean and now for the special case of `==(::Fix, x)` it would return a function:
   
   ```julia
   function update_y!(A, f)
       if f == A.f
           # y was already calculated with this f
           return
       end
   
       A.f = f
       A.y = f(A.x)
   end    
   
   # Works
   update_y!(A, x->x%3)
   
   # Doesn't work?
   update_y!(A, _%3)
   ```

1. I don't think it works with generic functions? For example a user might expect `min(_^2, 1)` to mean `x->min(x^2, 1)` if (to them) a function argument doesn't make sense for `min`. But the `MethodError` will occur deeper, when `isless` is called.
   
   It seems quite bad to have the same syntax mean very different things depending on whether a function is generic.

1. From the preceding points it seems clear that it would make code hard to read: just to understand the syntax, the user must know which functions have a method that accepts a function as argument? 

1. It limits how the language can evolve in a backward-compatible way: we make `sin(_)+1` work using a fallback composition, but then we cannot define `sin(f)` in a later Julia version (to mean something else than composition). That doesn't sound like a real problem for `sin` but think of other cases. We cannot define `abs(f)`, `minimum(f)`, `collect(f)`... That's a very weird restriction to put on the future development of the language.

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":415,"created_at":"2017-12-08T19:10:10Z","updated_at":"2022-12-30T11:04:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/24990","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR addresses #554, #5571, and #22710 by "currying" underscores in function calls like `f(_,y)` into anonymous function expressions `x -> f(x,y)`.  (Note that `_.foo` works and turns into `x -> x.foo` since it is equivalent to a `getfield` call, and `_[i]` works and turns into `x -> x[i]`, since it is equivalent to a `getindex(_,i)` call.)

This will help us get rid of functions like `equalto` (#23812) or `occursin` (#24967), is useful for "destructuring" as discussed in #22710, and should generally be convenient in lots of cases to avoid having to explicitly do `x -> f(x,y)`.

Some simplifying design decisions that I made:

* The currying is "tight", i.e. it only converts the immediately surrounding function call into a lambda, [as suggested by](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22710#issuecomment-314259250) @JeffBezanson (and [as in Scala](https://www.scala-lang.org/files/archive/spec/2.11/06-expressions.html#placeholder-syntax-for-anonymous-functions)).   So, e.g. `f(g(_,y))` is equivalent to `f(x -> g(x,y))`.   (Note that something like `find(!(_ in c), y)` will work fine, because the `!` operator works on functions; you can also use `_ ∉ c`.)  Any other rule seems hard to make comprehensible and consistent.

* ~~Only a single underscore is allowed.  `f(_,_)` throws an error: this case seems ambiguous to me (do you want `x -> f(x,x)` or `x,y -> f(x,y)`?), so it seemed better to punt on this for now.  We can always add a meaning for multiple underscores later.~~ Similar to Scala, multiple underscores are converted into multiple arguments in the order they appear.  e.g. `f(_,y,_)` is equivalent to `(x,z) -> f(x,y,z)`.  See [rationale below](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-350879561).

The implementation is pretty trivial.  If people are in favor, I will add
- [x] Tests
- [x] Documentation
- [ ] Fix interaction with broadcasting `f.(x, _)`
- [ ] Fix interaction with keyword arguments `f(x; y=_)`
- [ ] Support chained comparisons, e.g. `3 ≤ _ ≤ 10`, since they parse as a single expression?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/reactions","total_count":274,"+1":161,"-1":4,"laugh":0,"hooray":11,"confused":2,"heart":83,"rocket":9,"eyes":4},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1367859481","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-1367859481","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990","id":1367859481,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Rh-EZ","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-12-30T11:04:05Z","updated_at":"2022-12-30T11:04:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"To stay on-topic, it would be nice to summarize the counterarguments for the tight-binding rule in isolation (without composition fallback). Here's what I remember as the main issues:

1. Too limited use cases, not worth the additional syntax.
2.  `map(_ + 1 + _, v)` means `map((x, y) -> x + 1 + y, v)` but `map(_ - 1 - _, v)` means `map(x -> (y -> y - 1) - x, v)`.

Point 1 is subjective, but I think it's fair to say that it's unconvincing given the interest people have shown in this GitHub issue, and the amount of use-cases that can be found just in the standard library (see https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-1206335408).

Point 2 is a serious issue, but I see two reasonable solutions: a) disallow `_` in `+` calls with three or more arguments and b) implement `-(::Fix, x)` and `-(x, ::Fix)` as compositions (same idea as above, but implemented explicitly for this function, *not* as a fallback).

What are other arguments for people strongly opposed to the tight-binding rule?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1367859481/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567","id":749867970,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU3NDk4Njc5NzA=","number":38567,"title":"Constructor fails due to ambiguous convert in self-referencing struct ","user":{"login":"li1","id":9516121,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk1MTYxMjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/9516121?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1","html_url":"https://github.com/li1","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/li1/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2020-11-24T16:39:28Z","updated_at":"2022-11-17T08:32:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi, running the following code fails for me on
```
Julia Version 1.5.3
Commit 788b2c77c1 (2020-11-09 13:37 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (x86_64-apple-darwin18.7.0)
  CPU: Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-9750H CPU @ 2.60GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-9.0.1 (ORCJIT, skylake)
Environment:
  JULIA_PROJECT = /Users/malte/Documents/Dev/raicode
```
but also on 1.4, 1.3, and 1.6 nightly:

```
struct Inner{K,V} end
struct Passed{K,V} end

struct Outer{K,V}
    inner::Inner{K,V}
    nested_outer::Outer{Int,Int}

    function Outer{K,V}(::Passed{K,V}) where {K,V}
        return new{K,V}(Inner{K,V}())
    end
end

passed = Passed{Int,Int}()
outer = Outer{Int,Int}(passed)
```

It prints the following error message while executing the last line:
```
ERROR: LoadError: MethodError: convert(::Type{Inner{Int64,V}}, ::Inner{Int64,Int64}) is ambiguous. Candidates:
  convert(::Type{T}, x::T) where T>:Union{Missing, Nothing} in Base at missing.jl:68
  convert(::Type{T}, x::T) where T>:Nothing in Base at some.jl:33
  convert(::Type{T}, x::T) where T>:Missing in Base at missing.jl:67
  convert(::Type{T}, x) where T>:Nothing in Base at some.jl:34
  convert(::Type{T}, x::T) where T in Base at essentials.jl:171
  convert(::Type{T}, x) where T>:Missing in Base at missing.jl:69
  convert(::Type{T}, x) where T>:Union{Missing, Nothing} in Base at missing.jl:70
To resolve the ambiguity, try making one of the methods more specific, or adding a new method more specific than any of the existing applicable methods.
Stacktrace:
 [1] Outer{Int64,Int64}(::Passed{Int64,Int64}) at /Users/[...]/src/bug.jl:9
 [2] top-level scope at /Users/[...]/src/bug.jl:14
 [3] include(::String) at ./client.jl:457
 [4] top-level scope at REPL[1]:1
in expression starting at /Users/[...]/src/bug.jl:14
```

I expect it to create `Outer` without failing:
```
Outer{Int64,Int64}(Inner{Int64,Int64}(), #undef)
```

From the error message, it looks like the conversion is ambiguous, but I don't see why Julia shouldn't be able to figure out `V` for `Inner`.

Interestingly, the error does **not** occur if I change any of the following:
- only use one type argument (`K` instead of `{K,V}`)
- remove the self-referencing `nested_outer` field completely
- pick different types for `nested_outer` and `Passed (passed) / Outer (outer)`

For example, this would work:
```
struct Inner{K,V} end
struct Passed{K,V} end

struct Outer{K,V}
    inner::Inner{K,V}
    nested_outer::Outer{Int,Float64}

    function Outer{K,V}(::Passed{K,V}) where {K,V}
        return new{K,V}(Inner{K,V}())
    end
end

passed = Passed{Int,Int}()
outer = Outer{Int,Int}(passed)
```
Note that I changed `nested_outer`'s type to `Outer{Int,Float64}`, which doesn't match `Passed{Int,Int}`/`Outer{Int,Int}`.

Thanks for looking into it! ❤️ 

(also pinging @NHDaly who might be interested to follow along)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1318270000","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567#issuecomment-1318270000","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38567","id":1318270000,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5OkzQw","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-17T08:32:26Z","updated_at":"2022-11-17T08:32:26Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This seems to have been fixed, at least it works now on 1.8.2...","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1318270000/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":392,"created_at":"2017-12-08T19:10:10Z","updated_at":"2022-08-05T11:19:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/24990","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR addresses #554, #5571, and #22710 by "currying" underscores in function calls like `f(_,y)` into anonymous function expressions `x -> f(x,y)`.  (Note that `_.foo` works and turns into `x -> x.foo` since it is equivalent to a `getfield` call, and `_[i]` works and turns into `x -> x[i]`, since it is equivalent to a `getindex(_,i)` call.)

This will help us get rid of functions like `equalto` (#23812) or `occursin` (#24967), is useful for "destructuring" as discussed in #22710, and should generally be convenient in lots of cases to avoid having to explicitly do `x -> f(x,y)`.

Some simplifying design decisions that I made:

* The currying is "tight", i.e. it only converts the immediately surrounding function call into a lambda, [as suggested by](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22710#issuecomment-314259250) @JeffBezanson (and [as in Scala](https://www.scala-lang.org/files/archive/spec/2.11/06-expressions.html#placeholder-syntax-for-anonymous-functions)).   So, e.g. `f(g(_,y))` is equivalent to `f(x -> g(x,y))`.   (Note that something like `find(!(_ in c), y)` will work fine, because the `!` operator works on functions; you can also use `_ ∉ c`.)  Any other rule seems hard to make comprehensible and consistent.

* ~~Only a single underscore is allowed.  `f(_,_)` throws an error: this case seems ambiguous to me (do you want `x -> f(x,x)` or `x,y -> f(x,y)`?), so it seemed better to punt on this for now.  We can always add a meaning for multiple underscores later.~~ Similar to Scala, multiple underscores are converted into multiple arguments in the order they appear.  e.g. `f(_,y,_)` is equivalent to `(x,z) -> f(x,y,z)`.  See [rationale below](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-350879561).

The implementation is pretty trivial.  If people are in favor, I will add
- [x] Tests
- [x] Documentation
- [ ] Fix interaction with broadcasting `f.(x, _)`
- [ ] Fix interaction with keyword arguments `f(x; y=_)`
- [ ] Support chained comparisons, e.g. `3 ≤ _ ≤ 10`, since they parse as a single expression?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/reactions","total_count":256,"+1":147,"-1":4,"laugh":0,"hooray":9,"confused":2,"heart":81,"rocket":9,"eyes":4},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1206335408","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-1206335408","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990","id":1206335408,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5H5zew","user":{"login":"knuesel","id":2412819,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0MTI4MTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2412819?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel","html_url":"https://github.com/knuesel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/knuesel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-05T11:19:14Z","updated_at":"2022-08-05T11:19:14Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I also wonder why the "one call" rule would not be viable. The proposed "one call + operators" rule covers more cases but I think it chases the wrong metric. Same mistake I made in my comparison (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-605531476), and same mistake in @LilithHafner's (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-1205785095): we were looking at the proportion of cases that *could* be replaced with `_`. But *should* they? That's a better question. So now I think code more complex than a single call is better left using `x->...`.

Some are concerned about inconsistencies with `in` and the special casing of operators like `|>`. The "one call" rule doesn't have these problems (though it doesn't magically solve the issue with `1 + _ + 1` vs `1 - _ - 1`).

For readability, some are concerned that it's easy to overlook the `_` which completely changes the meaning of the code. With the "one call" rule it's not a problem since the effect of `_` is purely local.


@stevengj showed how the "one call" rule is already useful, using some examples from Base and standard libraries (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-752110486). Here are the same examples but also showing how it looks using `_`:

|Current code|With "one call" rule|Comment|
|-|-|-|
|`(K, V) -> WeakKeyDict{K, V}`|`WeakKeyDict{_,_}`| if we treat a `Foo{_,_}` as a "call"|
|`x->x isa Integer`|`_ isa Integer`||
|`t -> t[i]`|`_[i]`||
|`i->unalias(A,i)`|`unalias(A, _)`||
|`f -> !from(f, m)`|`!from(_,m)`||
|`frame -> !frame.from_c`|`!_.form_c`||
|`x -> x[1]` and `x -> x[2]` and `x -> x[4]`|`_[1]`|this pattern is used in many places|
|`a->is_expr(a, :kw)`|`is_expr(_, :kw)`||
|`a->isa(a,Symbol)`|`isa(_, Symbol)`||
|`x->2x`|`2*_`||
|`n -> n%3`|`_%3`||
|`x -> x^2`|`_^2`||
|`C->eachindex(style, C)`|`eachindex(style, _)`||
|`i -> fieldtype(T, i)`|`fieldtype(T, _)`||
|`i -> i > 0`|`_ > 0`|used many times|
|`_bool(f) = x->f(x)::Bool`|`_bool(f) = f(_)::Bool`||
|`i->4<=i<=6`|`4 <= _ <= 6`|if we treat chained comparisons as a single "call"|
|`i -> 2*i`|`2*_`||
|`e -> !(e isa LineNumberNode)`|`!(_ isa LineNumberNode)`||
|`n->getfield(a, n)`|`getfield(a, _)`|
|`its->view(A, its...)`|`view(A, _...)`||
|`i->!(i in dims)`|`!(_ in dims)` or `_ ∉ dims`|
|`x->x[1,1]`|`_[1,1]`||
|`d->I[d]`|`I[_]`|used several times|
|`i -> i == dims`|`_ == dims`||
|`(i,j) -> i:j`|`_:_`||
|`(i,s,j) -> i:s:j`|`_:_:_`||
|`r->convert(OrdinalRange{Int, Int}, r)`|`convert(OrdinalRange{Int, Int}, _)`||
|`x -> startswith(x, entryfile * "_")`|`startswith(_, entryfile * "_")`||
|`i->bytes[i]`|`bytes[_]`||
|`x -> x[1]`|`_[1]`||
|`I -> I == EltypeUnknown()`|`_ == EltypeUnknown()`||
|`io->countlines(io, eol = eol)`|`countlines(_, eol = eol)`||
|`io->readuntil(io, args...; kw...)`|`readuntil(_, args...; kw...)`||
|`io->read!(io, a)`|`read!(_, a)`||
|`io->read(io, T)`|`read(_, T)`|and many similar examples with `io` arguments|
|`r->convert(AbstractUnitRange{Int}, r)`|`convert(AbstractUnitRange{Int}, _)`||
|`(K, V) -> IdDict{K, V}`|`IdDict{_, _}`||
|`a->T(a...)`|`T(_...)`||
|`a->f(a...)`|`f(_...)`||
|`x->first(x)`|`first(_)`||
|`x -> x <: Number`|`_ <: Number`||
|`x->x>=0`|`_ >= 0`||
|`x->isa(x,Union{Tuple,Pair})`|`isa(_,Union{Tuple,Pair})`||
|`c -> bind(c, t)`|`bind(_, t)`||
|`i->put!(c, i)`|`put!(_, i)`||
|`i->c[i]`|`c[_]`||
|`arg->show(io, arg)`|`show(io, _)`||
|`(rng, offset)->rng .+ offset`|`_ .+ _`||
|`(rng, offset)->rng .- offset`|`_ .- _`||
|`p -> triplet(p)`|`triplet(_)`||
|`p -> platforms_match(p, platform)`|`platforms_match(_, platform)`||
|`x -> occursin("libstdc++", x)`|`occursin("libstdc++", _)`|and several other `occursin` calls|
|`(io, slice) -> _show_nonempty(io, slice, prefix)`|`_show_nonempty(_, _, prefix)`|
|`v -> v-1`|`_-1`||
|`a->size(a, 1)`|`size(_, 1)`||
|`X->eachindex(IndexLinear(), X)`|`eachindex(IndexLinear(), _)`|
|`(io)->escape_string(io, s, esc; keep = keep)`|`escape_string(_, s, esc; keep = keep)`||
|`(args...)->f(mixedargs[1], T, args...)`|`f(mixedargs[1], T, _...)`||
|`(args...)->f(T, args...)`|`f(T, _...)`||
|`(r, i) -> randn(r, T, i)`|`randn(_, T, _)`|used several times|

There's redundancy in this list but maybe that's also the point: the "one call" rule covers several common cases where its use is straightforward and which users can quickly learn to recognize. A more complex rule like "one call + operators" will cover more cases that look different and complex. These should often use `->` instead.

the following:

```julia
julia> open("test.txt", "w") do file
           write(file, "line1\n")
           run(pipeline(`echo line2`, file))
           write(file, "line3\n")
       end;

shell> cat test.txt
line2
line1
line3
```

Since all writes are made with synchronous methods in the same task, it seems reasonable to expect they will happen in program order.

Discussion on Discourse: https://discourse.julialang.org/t/pipeline-writes-out-of-order/40351

```
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.4.1
Commit 381693d3df* (2020-04-14 17:20 UTC)
Platform Info:

  LIBM: libopenlibm

discussion around https://discourse.julialang.org/t/typeconst-proposal/75953/19. The current warning does not make clear exactly what is allowed and not when redefining consts, and so conveys the impression that it is wrong to redefine consts at all (if so, why is it allowed?). As I understand it, it's OK to redefine consts as long as you redefine the functions that use them. Is it the case? If yes, the proposed PR clarifies that. 1. `@less sin(1)`
2. Press Ctrl-C
3. Observe weird state: Julia shows the prompt, but less is still running.
4. Press `q` to exit less. Julia hangs.
5. Press Ctrl-C: Julia crashes.

A workaround for this particular case is to start Julia with `PAGER='less -K'`, to make `less` exit on Ctrl-C.

Related issue: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/14032

1. Execute `@less sin(1)`. It shows the code in the pager.
2. Press `Ctrl-C`. It doesn't exit the page.
3. Quickly press `Ctrl-C` a few times. It exits the pager with `WARNING: Force throwing a SIGINT`.
4. Press `Enter`. It looks like the REPL is in a corrupt state: it shows "(END)" at the bottom. The `less` instance is still running and reading from the keyboard.
5. Press `q`. The prompt doesn't look quite right.
6. Press `Ctrl-C` then `Enter`. Julia crashes.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1051060583/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
